key_matchups: Molina vs Austria on Argentina's right side
Lautaro Martinez vs Posch and Schlager in the box
Wanner vs Molina on the left edge of the area
phases.summary: The opening phase is characterized by both teams pressing aggressively rather than settling into a passive block. Argentina create the sharper early attacks through Molina’s right-back involvement and Messi’s passing, though the penalty sequence interrupts the rhythm and leaves the game disjointed. Austria then enjoy a stronger spell, with Danso helping them pin Argentina back and force fouls as Argentina struggle to play out cleanly.

formations_blocks: Austria in a fierce high press
Austria with a compact, energetic midfield block
Argentina forced into a counter-attacking phase
Argentina struggling to play through the middle
key_matchups: Messi vs Austria’s defensive work rate / pressing structure
Lautaro Martínez combining with Messi against the Austrian back line
Marcel Sabitzer arriving from midfield and testing Argentina
phases.summary: Austria set the tone with an aggressive press and a very compact midfield, making it difficult for Argentina to build through central areas. Argentina’s main route became direct transitions to Messi and Lautaro, with their link-up gradually improving before Messi’s 38th-minute goal gave them the lead.】【。outputs

formations_blocks: Austrian attempts to penetrate against Argentina's defensive block
Argentina content to see out the half with a 1-0 lead
phases.summary: Argentina went into half-time 1-0 up and appeared comfortable managing the game rather than chasing a second goal. Austria were able to circulate the ball and switch play, but the commentary stresses that they could not find a way through Argentina’s back line, while Lionel Scaloni’s side looked happy to protect the lead until the break.

formations_blocks: Austria pressing high and winning turnovers in midfield
Argentina in a more sedate, controlled possession phase
Austria defending the box against Argentina's free-kick/cross situations
adjustments: Laimer and Sabitzer continuing to combine on Austria's left
Schmid and Wanner offering more hustle and presence between the lines at the start of the second half
Argentina increasing tempo slightly after halftime to gain a grip of the game
Messi dropping/roaming into central spaces to receive in behind, though caught offside
key_matchups: Emi Martínez vs Austria's attacking pressure/crosses
Cristian Romero vs Austria attack/fitness management
phases.summary: Austria are still leaning on their left-sided Laimer-Sabitzer connection and aggressive midfield pressing to win territory, but they’re struggling to turn that into enough box entries or clear shooting chances. At the start of the second half, Schmid and Wanner add energy and pockets of creativity, while Argentina respond by trying to slow the game and reassert control through possession and set pieces, with Messi briefly threatening in behind before being caught offside. The phase is defined more by territory, fouls, and restarts than by open attacking flow.

formations_blocks: Argentina deep defensive shape / controlled low block
Austria committing bodies forward and later resorting to direct balls and crosses into the box
adjustments: Argentina brought on Leandro Paredes to beef up midfield
Argentina replaced Facundo Medina with Nicolás Tagliafico after Medina’s booking
Austria introduced Carney Chukwuemeka to add attacking threat
key_matchups: Cristian Danso and Austria’s defenders vs Argentina’s late attacks
Austria’s attacking players vs Argentina’s block in the box
phases.summary: Argentina spent the closing stages protecting their lead with a more conservative midfield setup, then freshened the back line after Medina’s yellow. Austria tried to inject urgency with a new attacker and increasingly direct play, but their efforts were repeatedly met by blocks and resistance in the box. Messi’s late goal after an Austrian turnover underlined the gap between Argentina’s control and Austria’s lack of cutting edge.
